UX/UI Product Designer with 5+ years of experience designing SaaS, fintech, and B2B platforms in complex international environments. Recognized for leading end-to-end design projects and contributing to strategic financial applications by aligning product teams around a user-centered vision.

Looking for Product Design roles where user impact is at the core of decision-making.

Originally from Paris, France and now based in Dallas, United States, I bring a global perspective to my work. Outside of design, I'm a dedicated runner, actively involved in volunteer community and a mother cat.

Finance · CACIB · 2023–2024

Designing a cash management
platform from scratch —
zero-error environment.

At CACIB, I owned the entire design of a B2B application for back offices and treasurers — anticipating daily positions, managing cash flows, and replacing an obsolete legacy system. First and only designer in a low agile maturity environment, embedded in a team that had never worked with a designer before.

Role
Solo Product Designer
Timeline
6 months · 2.5d/week
Team
PO · BA · Dev · PM
Tools
Figma · FigJam · Teams
0+
Screens delivered
ready for testing
0
Full day
shadowing
0
User research
integrated
Problem

Fragmented tools. Unreliable data. No product vision.

Back offices and treasurers were managing daily cash positions by juggling multiple disconnected applications, macro-driven Excel files, swift messages, emails and phone calls. Data wasn't reliable. Any discrepancy could trigger serious financial consequences — and there was no integrated way to catch errors before they cascaded.

The existing tool offered no consolidated view of cash flows, no communication layer between stakeholders, and no way to find critical information quickly. The deeper problem was structural: there was no product vision. The business had been building screens feature by feature, sprint by sprint, without a coherent UX logic.

"In a cash management context, clarity isn't a UX principle — it's a risk management requirement."
0→1B2B FintechFirst DesignerLow Agile MaturityZero-error
01 — Discovery

Warm-up & Vision Alignment

The first step wasn't to sketch — it was to listen and observe. I started with a structured warm-up phase: a workshop to align different project visions across business stakeholders, then a session to define features and identify first lots with estimates.

In parallel, I built a shared lexicon — a living document translating banking terms into design vocabulary accessible to everyone. In an environment this technical, the absence of a common language is the first source of misunderstandings. Getting alignment on words before alignment on screens was a deliberate, strategic choice.

Warm-up workshop — product vision alignment & roadmap definition

Co-design sessions — objectives, vision produit, lots & découpage

02 — Research

Shadowing & Workflow Co-design

I conducted a full-day shadowing session with back office teams — a deep immersion into their daily reality to observe actual practices, identify real friction points, and understand what users genuinely do versus what they say they do.

That day was decisive. It surfaced unformulated needs, invisible workarounds that never appeared in specs, and deeply embedded habits that needed to be respected rather than disrupted. I then facilitated co-design workshops with 6 users, the PO and 2 BAs over 3 hours in person — collectively building workflows from the ground up.

Shadowing 1J — experience map restitution + opportunities workshop

Co-design workshops — workflow validation, screen zoning, step-by-step overview

03 — Conception

Reconciliation, Automation & System Coherence

High-fidelity screens covered the full cash management lifecycle — from daily positions to reconciliation, cash flow management and alerts. Every feature was designed to reduce cognitive load for users operating in an environment where a single error has immediate financial consequences.

Reconciliation — matching bank transactions so the cash balance reflects the actual account — was the most complex use case. The solution: accessible table creation, pre-filtered data, quick-view of daily positions. Both light and dark modes delivered to match different trading floor environments. Developers integrated from day one — continuously, not at handoff.

Wireframes & mockups — reconciliation workflow, light & dark mode

Reconciliation — unmatched table view, dark mode

Full reconciliation screen — aligned & compared view

04 — User Testing

Validating in Complexity

I set up a tight loop: test → insights → prioritization → design iteration → retest. This cadence progressively transformed the relationship with business teams: from end-of-sprint validation to permanent co-construction, where users were involved at every significant design decision.

Testing wasn't just a quality check. It was the most powerful tool I had to demonstrate design value to a team that had never seen it in action — making the case through evidence rather than argument.

User testing — insight prioritization & iteration tracking

05 — Delivery

Building a Learning Organization

Delivery wasn't an ending — it was the start of a continuous improvement cycle. The most important outcome wasn't the screens. It was ensuring the dynamics installed during the project would outlast it: the dailies, the retros, the co-design workshops — these became organizational rituals, not just project ones.

Three structural wins: design/business trust established · user testing normalized · systematic collaboration with clear rituals and shared ownership.

Delivery — implementation follow-up, design documentation

Reflection

What I learned designing for ultra-experts.

This project confronted me with one of the most demanding challenges in product design: designing for ultra-expert users in a dense, highly technical domain with non-negotiable reliability constraints. Every choice had to be justified by business logic, validated by real users, and technically sound.

Shadowing was the single most powerful tool on this project. Nothing replaces a full day spent observing what people actually do. That day shaped every major design decision that followed. I also learned that installing design culture in an organization that has never had it is a long game — every workshop, every testing session, every roadmap conversation was a brick laid toward an organization that understands product excellence isn't a luxury.

Coming soon

CACIB · Crédit Agricole CIB · 2024

Developing an international
high-value lending application —
from scratch.

First and only designer on a multi-app B2B ecosystem. A fragmented application used by the middle office to manage high-value international loans — no design culture, no shared vocabulary, no user-centered process. My mission: transform it into a coherent, scalable product while installing design thinking from scratch in 6 months.

Role
Solo Product Designer
Timeline
6 months · 4d/week
Team
PM · BA · PO · Dev Singapore
Tools
Figma · FigJam
0
Mockups produced
across all use cases
0
Months to ship
a coherent product
0
Countries in the
Loanscape ecosystem
Problem

A "Frankenstein" product. No designer. No culture.

The existing application was the result of years of feature accumulation with no designer involved. Journeys were broken, data couldn't be edited, business wording was inconsistent, and nothing was harmonized with the Loanscape Design System or connected to the program's other applications.

But beyond the interface, the deeper problem was structural: teams had never worked with a designer. No shared vocabulary, no user-centered process, no framework for design decisions. In a context where errors are not just frustrating but potentially fatal — triggering regulatory issues, financial losses or compliance failures — the cost of poor UX was enormous.

"The challenge wasn't only to design better screens. It was to build the conditions for design to exist, be trusted, and deliver value."
Investment Banking Multi-app Ecosystem First Designer Low Design Maturity Regulatory Constraints
01 — Discovery

Scoping & Embedding Design Culture

The first challenge wasn't understanding the product — it was being understood as a designer. I started with a full audit of the existing application: mapping broken journeys, identifying DS inconsistencies, and documenting cross-application dependencies.

At the same time, I set up the first dailies and weekly design reviews — rituals that didn't exist before — to create a shared rhythm between design, business, and tech. I also built a shared lexicon translating banking terms into design vocabulary. Getting alignment on words before alignment on screens was a deliberate, strategic choice.

Discovery — team organization, app schema, cross-app dependencies mapping

02 — Ideation

Workflows, Co-design & Strategic Vision

I facilitated co-design workshops with business teams to map and harmonize workflows across the full user journey. Using priority maps (effort/impact), I aligned the team on what mattered most. Each identified opportunity triggered a focused design cycle: hypothesis → rapid prototype → user test → data-driven decision.

My strategic vision: create coherence across the Loanscape multi-app ecosystem. Every workflow had to connect logically to adjacent applications — not just solve the immediate use case, but anticipate upstream and downstream interactions, reduce context-switching, and ensure users could navigate the full program without cognitive overload.

Workshops — retrospective GKF, backlog harmonization, journey alignment

Co-design — Lenderpool workflow, business processes, site map drafts

03 — Conception

High-Fidelity Screens & System Coherence

I designed 100+ high-fidelity screens covering all use cases. The real complexity wasn't visual — it was systemic. Every component decision had to account for three layers simultaneously: user experience, Design System consistency, and technical performance.

I worked exclusively with existing Loanscape DS components, avoiding custom patterns that could introduce inconsistencies or maintenance debt. Developers were integrated from day one — daily alignment on constraints meant what I designed could actually be built, and built correctly. The 4-eye validation process — requiring multiple approval levels with clear action tracking — was one of the most technically and experientially complex features.

Conception sprints — high-fidelity screens, design system components, cross-app coherence

Key use cases — pools additionnels & contractual amendment flows

04 — User Testing

Validating Journeys & Building Trust Through Evidence

I produced low-fidelity wireframes to test screen sequences and validate journeys before committing to high-fidelity work. Testing revealed participants significantly appreciated the new form experience — a high satisfaction score compared to the previous tool.

Key improvements validated: simplified form editing, harmonized navigation, clearer action hierarchy. One critical issue progressively resolved: the harmonization of business wording across profiles and applications. In a multi-app ecosystem, inconsistent terminology is not a minor UX problem — it creates confusion, errors, and loss of trust in the tool.

User testing wasn't just a design method here — it was a change management tool. Every test session brought business stakeholders face-to-face with real user behavior. Over time, teams started asking for tests rather than waiting to be convinced.

05 — Impact

Coherence where there was none.

Congratulations from sponsors during retros, PI Planning, and by email. Users described the new interface as "clear and uncluttered" — a striking contrast to what existed before.

Beyond the screens: the project shifted how teams thought about design — from a deliverable at the end of a sprint to a strategic asset embedded in the process. The foundations are in place: a coherent design language, harmonized workflows, a team that now understands what design can do.

Three structural wins: design/business trust established · user testing normalized · systematic collaboration with clear rituals and shared ownership.
Reflection

Designing in complexity — and making design stick.

This project was one of the most demanding of my career — not because of the interface complexity, but because of the human and organizational challenge of introducing design into a context that had never needed it.

I learned that in low-maturity environments, the first deliverable isn't a wireframe — it's credibility. Every workshop facilitated, every test session run, every developer conversation about performance and edge cases was an investment in the trust that made the design work possible.

Working on a multi-app ecosystem demands systemic thinking beyond individual screens. Every decision ripples. Every component choice has downstream consequences. Every workflow needs to connect — not just internally, but across applications, teams, and time.

Human Resources · Work4 · 2022–2023

Transforming an early MVP into
a scalable recruitment platform.

Work4 is a recruitment SaaS helping companies manage high-volume hiring. When I joined, the MVP and core design system were already in place. My role: extend the platform with scalable workflows covering automation, performance reporting, and campaign management — turning an early product into a production-ready platform used by hundreds of companies.

Role
Product Designer
Timeline
6 months · Agile
Team
PM · Lead Designer · Dev Back-end
Tools
Figma · Miro · Slack
0
Companies using
the platform
0
Reduction in
recruitment costs
0
Core workflows
designed end-to-end
Problem

A functional MVP with fragmented workflows.

Recruiters were managing complex, high-volume hiring campaigns through a functional but fragmented MVP. It lacked structured workflows, scalable interaction patterns, and clear performance visibility. This increased cognitive load, required manual coordination, and limited the ability to track candidates and make informed decisions.

The core challenge: transform a functional MVP into a scalable product capable of supporting complex recruitment operations — automation, data-driven reporting, and customizable campaign management.

SaaSRecruitmentAutomationDesign System0→1 Extension
01 — Discovery

Kick off, Workshops & Research

I worked closely with stakeholders to understand recruitment workflows, business constraints, and user needs. I facilitated workshops — an icebreaker Hopes & Fears session, then structured feature workshops on automation and candidate tracking — and collaborated with PMs to clarify scope and priorities.

This phase identified key opportunities: automate status-driven workflows, improve performance visibility, and design a scalable campaign structure. Three main client takeaways emerged: automation workflow, data dashboard visualization, and onboarding features with gamification.

Workshop — icebreaker, feature automation & candidate tracking, backlog planning

User research — 4 archetypes: Isabelle (RRH), Caroline (RH operationnelle), Julien (Commercial), Ronan (CSM)

02 — Ideation

Sitemap, Workflows & Automation Architecture

Building on the existing MVP and design system, I designed new workflows integrated into the platform architecture, aligned with user actions and business logic. I structured three main workflow streams: automation messaging, analytics/reporting, and workflow status customization.

Work4 offers 3 automation packages with different parameters. The customization capability — choosing triggers, message templates, and channels — was a key differentiator. I mapped all user stories and questions across these workflows before designing a single screen.

Sitemap V2 · Workflow automation · Workflow analytics

Automation workflows — 3 options: galerie, depuis un modèle, modifiable

03 — Conception

High-Fidelity Screens — Full Recruitment Lifecycle

I designed the complete platform interfaces covering campaign creation, automation, candidate management, and reporting. Working closely with engineers, I provided detailed specifications and supported development throughout implementation.

I also trained back-end developers on UX and frontend logic — producing a document "the fundamentals of working with back developers" — to improve collaboration and implementation quality.

Conception sprints — customization, no automation, automation screens

Delivery audit — candidate list, micro-macro annotations, dev accompaniment

04 — User Testing

Qualitative Testing on a Dozen Recruiters

A user path based on a prototype — from campaign creation through the form to candidate management and reporting — was tested on a panel of a dozen remote recruiters and decision-makers. This was followed by qualitative interviews with the company's sales staff to understand customer follow-up and contact points with users.

Key findings: recruiters significantly appreciated the new automation flow's clarity and the structured campaign wizard. The reporting dashboard required one more iteration to better surface actionable KPIs.

User testing — workflow performance analytics, reporting per recruiter & campaign

05 — Impact

A scalable product used by 400+ companies.

The platform successfully enabled Work4 to launch a scalable recruitment product used by over 400 companies, reducing recruitment costs by 25% while improving operational efficiency and visibility.

By transforming complex recruitment workflows into an intuitive and structured platform, the product empowered recruiters to manage campaigns more efficiently, make faster decisions, and scale their recruitment operations with confidence.

"This project transformed complex recruitment workflows into a scalable SaaS platform used by hundreds of companies."
Reflection

Designing for scale in a startup environment.

This project strengthened my ability to design scalable products and translate complex business processes into intuitive user experiences. I learned to balance user needs, business goals, and technical constraints while structuring a product foundation capable of supporting long-term growth.

The experience reinforced my role as a product designer contributing not only to interfaces, but to product structure and strategy — and the value of embedding design thinking from discovery through delivery.

Insurance · AXA Group · 2022–2023

Deploying a brand design system
for a global community —
30 countries.

AXA Group is a global insurance leader operating across 30+ countries. My role: contribute to the international adoption, deployment and evolution of their Design System — ensuring brand consistency, centralizing documentation, standardizing usage, and building a design community of 2000+ designers and developers.

Role
Product Designer
Timeline
1 year · 2d/week · Agile
Team
PM · Lead Designer · Dev · Brand Lead
Tools
Figma · Sketch · Contento · Trello
0
UI Kit downloads
worldwide
0
Countries using
the system
0
To build a full
site per entity
Problem

30 countries. No shared source of truth.

Design teams across AXA's 30+ country entities were working with fragmented visual libraries, inconsistent UI patterns, and no centralized reference for brand usage. Designers reinvented the wheel from one entity to the next, developers lacked clear specifications, and end users experienced a fragmented brand identity across digital touchpoints.

The core challenge: create a single source of truth — a living, scalable Design System that could support the entire AXA design community and accelerate product delivery at scale.

Design SystemDesignOpsScaleInsurance30 CountriesAccessibility
01 — Strategy

Three pillars: Creativity, Organization, Community.

The strategy was structured around three principles: be an aid to creativity (components + UI Kit + design templates), propose an operational organization (clear contribution paths, documentation, test protocols), and contribute together (community via Teams, user tests, newsletters).

I gathered needs through surveys, workshops, and weekly reporting — then benchmarked existing components and patterns across entities to identify gaps and harmonization opportunities.

Strategy — conception method, contribution paths, user test protocol

02 — Discovery

From audit to architecture.

I conducted a full audit of existing libraries across entities, mapped inconsistencies, and established the component architecture. Working with the PM, lead designer, and brand team, I defined the component hierarchy — from foundations (colors, typography, spacing, grid) to complex patterns (tables, forms, navigation, hero covers).

User test protocols were built to validate comprehension and usability of the system — not just the components, but the documentation itself. In a design system used by marketers and developers as much as designers, documentation clarity is a design problem.

Documentation — alerts component, implementation guides, newsletter community

03 — Conception

UI Kit — Full component library in Figma & Sketch.

I designed and documented UI components in both Figma and Sketch covering the full spectrum: Header, Alerts, Toast, Dialogs, Buttons, Footer, Figures, Tables, Images, Tooltips, Spinners, Scrollspy, Progress, and more. Each component was built with defined behaviors, micro-interactions, and usage examples.

Components were published on the AXA Design System website (designsystem.axa.com) via Contento CMS. I worked closely with developers to ensure design-to-code parity and contributed to verification of implementation across entities.

Figma UI Kit — component pages: Header, Alerts, Buttons, Tables, Data Picker...

AXA Design System website — components, homepage example, documentation

04 — Community & Testing

2000+ designers. One system. One community.

User testing was conducted before go-live on both components and documentation. Tests validated comprehension of naming, navigation, and implementation guides — with iterations after each round. I ran restitution sessions to close the loop with the design community.

A communication campaign was set up to drive adoption: newsletter, Teams community messages (extended to marketing teams), and COMEX presentations prepared and delivered. The community became a self-sustaining feedback loop — contributors proposing improvements, entities sharing adaptations.

05 — Impact

3000+ downloads. 1 week to build a site.

The Design System became a real design product with high added value, used by the entire AXA design community. 3000+ UI Kit downloads worldwide. Any entity can now build the main pages of a site in under one week — down from weeks of fragmented work.

By standardizing components, centralizing documentation, and building a collaborative community, the system empowered designers, developers, and marketers across 30 countries to create faster, more consistently, and with greater confidence.

"This project unified AXA's design language across 30 countries, enabling a global community of 2000+ to build faster and more consistently."
Reflection

A design system is never finished — it's maintained.

The biggest learning: a design system is not a deliverable. It's a product that needs continuous care, governance, and community. The technical work (components, documentation, implementation) is only half the challenge. The other half is human — convincing stakeholders, educating contributors, building habits.

I also learned that accessibility is not a feature to add at the end — it's a constraint that shapes every component decision from the start. Building it into the system from the foundation level was one of the most impactful contributions I made.
